text: 中华心血管病杂志 2025 年5 月第 53 卷第 5 期 Chin J Cardiol, May 2025, Vol. 53, No. 5 · 465 · microvascular resistance，IMR）：IMR 定义为冠脉充 血状态下，使用压力/温度传感器导丝测量的Pd 除 以最大充血期平均传导时间倒数 ［77］，IMR≥25 提示 微循环阻力升高，考虑存在CMD。与CFR 不同， IMR 与心外膜血管无关，IMR 不受心率、血压等血 流动力学指标及基线血流量的影响，可重复率很 高，因此，是目前反映冠脉微循环功能的主要指标。 IMR 可特异性评价微血管功能，准确预测急性心肌 梗死血运重建后的心室重构、心肌存活度及心功能 恢复 ［78‑79］。同时，越来越多的证据表明，IMR或血运 重建后IMR 可以预测心脏移植术后、Takotsubo 综 合征、急性心肌梗死和稳定性心绞痛患者的心血管 事件 ［78‑81］。在STEMI 患者中，与造影或CFR 相比， IMR 与微血管功能、左心室功能和临床结局的联系 更加紧密 ［82］。 3.充血性微血管阻力：是一项基于多普勒和压 力导丝的测量技术，是在最大充血状态下，狭窄冠 脉远端压力与远端平均峰值流速之比 ［83］。在急性 心肌梗死患者中，充血性微血管阻力在评估心肌微 血管损伤及预测死亡和心力衰竭发作再入院方面 优于CFR ［84］。 4. 微血管阻力储备（microvascular resistance reserve，MRR）：MRR 是一种独立于心外膜冠脉病 变和血流动力学改变、通过连续热稀释法量化冠脉 微循环功能的指标，具有高度的准确性和可重复 性 ［85］。在心绞痛伴非阻塞性冠脉疾病患者中， MRR 值>2.7 可排除CMD，而<2.1 则提示CMD 可 能 ［86］。另有研究显示，PCI术后MRR在STEMI患者 中具有预测预后的价值，MRR 值<1.25 与较高的全 因死亡率或心力衰竭住院风险相关 ［87］。MRR 作为 一种新的微血管评估指标，在心血管疾病诊治中展 现了可观的应用前景。 此外，还有一些新的评估方法处于研发阶段， 如基于冠脉造影的IMR，这些评估方法尚需进一步 研究其在CMD诊断中的价值。 术后冠脉功能学评估 冠脉功能学评估除了决策冠脉病变是否需要 干预外，还可以在PCI 术后识别残余功能性病变， 同时区分残余局灶性和弥漫性功能性病变，优化 PCI ［88‑90］，其对不同病变的评判标准可参考表2 ［91］。 1. PCI 术后冠脉功能学指标与预后的相关性： PCI 术后FFR 越高，不良临床结局的发生率越低。 2011 年的一项研究显示，药物洗脱支架置入术后 FFR>0.90 相较于FFR≤0.90 的患者术后1 年临床不 良事件发生率显著降低 ［92］。一项纳入7 470例患者 的荟萃分析显示，PCI 术后FFR≥0.90 与再次PCI 和 临床不良事件的风险降低显著相关 ［93］。而 DKCRUSH Ⅶ多中心大样本的回顾性研究显示，置 入药物洗脱支架的患者中，术后FFR>0.88 者术后 3 年临床预后显著优于≤0.88 的患者 ［94］。另一项纳 入574 例PCI 患者（664 个病变）的术后长期随访研 究结果证实，FFR≤0.86对PCI术后临床不良事件的 预测较为精准 ［88］。此外，一项研究对5 869 条接受 PCI 的冠脉的相关数据进行荟萃分析，结果显示药 物洗脱支架置入术后预测不良事件的最佳FFR 值 为0.86 ［95］。对于NHPR，DEFINE PCI 研究显示，PCI 术后iFR≥0.95 与患者预后改善显著相关 ［89］。一项 关于跨支架压力梯度的研究显示，PCI 术后低支架 压力梯度（ΔFFR<0.04 或ΔFFR/10 mm<0.009）预示 着更低的临床事件发生率 ［96］。结合上述循证医学 证据，建议当PCI 术后FFR≤0.86，ΔFFR≥0.04 或 ΔFFR/10 mm≥0.009时进行靶血管的术后优化。 2. PCI 术后冠脉功能学欠佳的潜在机制及优 化策略：目前，PCI 术后冠脉功能学结果欠佳的潜 在机制包括： （1）支架相关因素所致PCI 术后功能 学结果欠佳，包括支架膨胀不全、支架贴壁不良、斑 块突入

PCI 术后冠脉功能学欠佳的潜在机制及优 化策略：目前，PCI 术后冠脉功能学结果欠佳的潜 在机制包括： （1）支架相关因素所致PCI 术后功能 学结果欠佳，包括支架膨胀不全、支架贴壁不良、斑 块突入、支架内血栓、病变覆盖范围不足、支架尺寸 选择不当、支架边缘夹层； （2）靶血管因素所致PCI 术后功能学结果欠佳，包括残余弥漫病变、未处理 表2 PCI术后常用冠状动脉功能学评估方法对不同病变的评判标准 ［91］ 病变类型 局部病变 串联病变 弥漫病变 FFR 术后靶血管或支架置入处FFR 值 明显跳跃 未处理串联病变处FFR 值明显 跳跃 压力导丝回撤过程中支架内FFR 值渐进性减低 NHPR 支架部位或治疗血管内其他部位的 iFR/RFR/DFR值明显跳跃 未处理的串联病变处iFR/RFR/DFR 值明显 跳跃 压力导丝回撤过程中支架内iFR/RFR/DFR 值渐进性减低 FCA 支架部位或治疗血管内其他部位的QFR 或其他 虚拟FFR值明显跳跃 未处理的串联病变处QFR 或其他虚拟FFR 明显 跳跃 压力回撤过程中支架内QFR 或其他虚拟FFR 值 渐进性减低 注：PCI 为经皮冠状动脉介入治疗，FFR 为血流储备分数，NHPR 为非充血性压力指数，iFR 为瞬时无波形比值，RFR 为静息全周期比值， DFR为舒张期无充血比值，FCA为功能性冠状动脉造影，QFR为定量血流分数

text: · 466 · 中华心血管病杂志 2025 年5 月第 53 卷第 5 期 Chin J Cardiol, May 2025, Vol. 53, No. 5 的局部病变。建议充分结合冠脉功能学和影像学 技术进行PCI 术后优化，PCI 术后冠脉功能学指导 的优化策略可参考图4 ［91］。 冠脉功能学与影像学 1.结合功能学与影像学的预后分层：心肌缺血 与冠脉不稳定斑块均为影响患者预后的不良因素， 均可导致心血管不良事件。不稳定斑块又称易损 斑块，是独立于血流动力学因素的预后预测因子。 在无功能性缺血的患者中，不稳定斑块特征的数量 与预后呈正相关。冠脉功能学在评估缺血方面具 有优势，然而，对于评估斑块性质和不稳定斑块具 有明显的局限性，而冠脉影像学则在这方面具有一 定优势。因此，结合功能学与影像学可以更精准地 预测患者预后。 2. 冠脉功能学结合影像学共同指导PCI： PREVENT 研究提示，FFR 阴性的病变如果合并影 像学提示的高危斑块，预防性PCI 可能也有获 益 ［97］。因此，在决策PCI 时功能学与影像学融合可 以更好地评估病变，进一步优化介入治疗效果。 FLAVOUR Ⅲ（NCT06218485）、DEBuT‑LRP （NCT04765956）等研究将进一步证实冠脉功能学 结合影像学共同指导冠心病介入治疗的有效性。 3. 功能学与影像学指导PCI 策略的比较：导管 室中冠脉功能学通常用于决策是否需要血运重建， 而影像学常用于优化PCI 策略。然而，由于各种限 制（如延长手术时间、操作繁琐、经济问题等）很难 同时使用2 种有创评估手段，经常需要用1 种工具 完成PCI 决策以及优化支架置入的全过程。 FLAVOUR 研究显示，FFR 指导的PCI 策略在24 个 月的临床结局与生活质量方面与IVUS指导的介入 治疗策略相似，同时减少了20.9% 的支架置入 ［98］。 冠脉功能学与影像学指导的介入治疗策略均可应 用于临界病变，但卫生经济学效益方面FFR 略胜 一筹。 因此，建议对病变进行缺血和斑块性质的功能 影像学综合评估，可以进一步改善患者预后，也可 进行更精准的预后风险分层。 冠脉功能学的性别差异 男性与女性的生理差异导致冠心病在不同性 别中具有不同的表现。女性的冠心病发病时间较 晚、斑块总负荷较小、高危斑块较少、心外膜血管病 变相对较轻。冠脉功能学评估在女性冠心病患者 的诊治中具有不可替代的价值。 1.女性心外膜血管的评估：肉眼评估的冠脉造 影直径狭窄率具有明显的局限性，相比男性，女性 的冠脉狭窄程度更容易被高估。有研究显示，在女 性临界病变中，FFR 指导策略相较IVUS 指导策略 可以减少33.4% 的介入治疗，而在男性中仅减少 22.5%。同时，在临界病变中，即使女性接受有创治 疗的比例（40.8%）低于男性（47.9%），但临床事件 发生率却低于男性（女性的不良事件发生率为 2.4%，男性为4.5%），表明过于积极的有创治疗可 能在女性患者中的获益有限，女性可能在功能学指 导中获益更多 ［99］。在女性心外膜血管的评估工具 中，可以首选FFR，此外，iFR可能有 与FFR相当的临床价值 ［100‑101］。 2. 女性微血管功能评估：相比 男性，微血管功能障碍在女性中更 常见。女性的CFR 明显低于男性。 这种差异主要是由于女性静息状态 的血流量较男性高，而充血状态的 血流量无差异。另外，与男性不同， 女性CFR 低与临床结局无关 ［102］。 CFR 在评估女性微血管功能和预测 预后方面价值有限。相比之下， IMR 可以更准确地评估微血管功能 障碍。 综上，冠脉功能学在女性心外 膜血管以及微血管功能评估方面均 PCI：经皮冠状动脉介入治疗 图4 PCI术后功能学指导的优化策略

doc_id: guidelines/冠状动脉功能学临床应用专家共识(1).pdf
source: Guideline: 冠状动脉功能学临床应用专家共识(1)
text: 中华心血管病杂志 2025 年5 月第 53 卷第 5 期 Chin J Cardiol, May 2025, Vol. 53, No. 5 · 467 · 有重要的临床价值，FFR 在心外膜血管评估中证据 较多，可以避免不必要的血运重建；CFR 在女性微 血管评估方面作用有限，建议将IMR作为首选评估 方式。 随着技术的发展，冠脉功能学的评估形式不再 局限于压力导丝，其应用也不再局限于决策是否进 行PCI，可以通过多种方式全方位评估，制定全面 的介入治疗策略。理想的冠脉功能学指导介入治 疗策略，首先，需要评估功能性狭窄的严重程度，利 用压力回撤曲线分析功能性狭窄的分布。然后，可 以结合虚拟支架技术选择最佳位置和支架尺寸。 最后，利用术后指标优化PCI结果（图5）。 冠脉功能学评估的循证医学证据日积月累，其 评估形式日趋多样化，多项大型临床研究也正在进 行，例如FLAVOUR Ⅱ ［103］、DEFINE GPS、iLARDI、 PRIME‑FFR 等，这些研究将为冠脉功能学指导介 入治疗策略增添更多重要的循证医学证据。未来 会出现更加简便、实用、高效的冠脉功能学评估手 FFR：血流储备分数，NHPR：非充血性压力指数，IVUS：血管内超声，OCT：光学相干断层成像，NIRS：近红外光谱，VH‑IVUS：虚拟组织学 成像IVUS，PCI：经皮冠状动脉介入治疗 图5 功能学指导制定介入治疗策略流程图

text: an important risk factor affecting the occurrence and progression of coronary artery disease (CAD). However, the dysbiosis on the tongue in patients with CAD is still unclear, and whether the oral alteration caused by these disorders can be identified by other tools for CAD diagnosis needs to be further explored. Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) is characterized as high spectral resolution, broad spectral range, and superior spatial resolution. Hyperspectral images contain high-dimensional data that generally require machine learning algorithms for feature identification and model construction. Therefore, this study aims to investigate the variation of tongue microbiota and the effectiveness of HSI models in CAD diagnosis. Methods Between 2023 and 2024, we prospectively approached 276 patients with chest pain and exhibiting risk for CAD who underwent coronary artery angiography (CAG). And 190 patients were enrolled in this study. Tongue dorsum swabs were collected for subsequent 16sRNA sequencing and microbiome analysis. Tongue dorsum features were extracted from hyperspectral images. The HSI analysis incorporated a total of 4750 hyperspectral images from all patients. All images are divided into training set (N = 2555), internal test set (N = 1095) and external test set (N = 1095). A total of 31 models were constructed. 30 single machine learning algorithms were used to construct and test the CAD prediction models. Furthermore, the best performing fusion model was established. The efficacy of the model was evaluated employing several metrics, including area under the curve (AUC), decision curve analysis (DCA), calibration curve, accuracy (ACC), sensitivity (SE), specificity (SP), positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV) and F1 score. BMC Cardiovascular Disorders (2025) 25:102 Introduction Coronary artery disease (CAD) remains the leading cause of mortality and chronic disability within the spectrum of cardiovascular diseases worldwide [1]. Recent stud­ ies have increasingly highlighted the close relationship between oral health and CAD [2, 3]. Dysbiosis within the oral microbiota constitutes a significant contributing factor to the pathogenesis of oral diseases, a significantly increased risk of cardiovascular disease and adverse car­ diovascular events among those with poor oral health [4]. Previous research has substantiated alterations in the oral microbiota associated with diseases such as diabe­ tes and CAD [5]. Additionally, the presence of common pathogenic bacteria from the oral cavity was identified in arterial plaques and within the arterial intima [6–8]. These oral pathogens facilitate the development and advancement of atherosclerotic plaques by mechanisms including the induction of immune-mediated inflamma­ tion and the disruption of metabolic homeostasis [2]. The dorsal tongue harbors a substantial microbial community and is integral to oral health [9]. However, it is unclear whether alterations in the microbiota colonized on the dorsal tongue are associated with CAD. Therefore, it is necessary to explore more alterations of microbiota on the tongue dorsum to further clarify the oral microbiome dysbiosis in CAD patients. Several facial features, such as alopecia, earlobe crease, and xanthelasmata, have long been associated with an increased risk of CAD [10, 11]. Additionally, the integra­ tion of facial imaging with deep learning algorithms has proven to be an effective method for screening patients with CAD [12, 13]. These studies suggest that superficial charac

text: tionally, the integra­ tion of facial imaging with deep learning algorithms has proven to be an effective method for screening patients with CAD [12, 13]. These studies suggest that superficial characteristics may serve as non-invasive predictors of CAD risk. However, previous studies have predominantly focused on alterations in facial skin characteristics, which may be subject to the influence of diverse ethnic skin tones. Notably, oral hyperspectral imaging (HSI) consti­ tutes a high-precision classification technique by offering comprehensive spectral information for each pixel within an image [14]. Thus, oral HSI enables a thorough char­ acterization and enhanced sensing perspective of target objects by utilizing its high spectral resolution, broad spectral range, and superior spatial resolution [15–18]. Previous studies have confirmed that HSI enables disease diagnosis primarily by identifying characteristic spectra reflected from the body surface or the tissue, such as the diagnosis of diabetic foot by characteristic spectral alter­ ations of hemoglobin on the skin surface. Therefore, large discrepancies in skin color on the body surface may have implications for the recognition of characteristic spectra [19, 20]. The utilization of oral HSI could effectively miti­ gate the impact of varying skin tones on superficial skin features. Additionally, modifications in the microbiota of the dorsal tongue exert an influence on its morphology, moisture levels, coloration, and other characteristics [21]. These alterations can be assessed through dorsal tongue imaging techniques. Therefore, harnessing the advan­ tages of oral HSI can facilitate the identification of addi­ tional surface characteristics relevant to the alteration of microbiota on the tongue dorsum. Therefore,

Therefore, we posited that significant alteration of microbiota on the dorsal tongue can be observed in CAD patients and surface characteristics on the dorsal tongue can be reflected in hyperspectral images. This study aimed to clarify the variations of microbiota on the dorsal tongue in CAD patients and conduct multiple machine learning algorithms to acquire and incorporate tongue features for detecting CAD. Methods Study design and participants The study consisted of two phases. In phase one, we prospectively enrolled 211 chest pain patients in Ren­ min Hospital of Wuhan University to develop and test the CAD models (between July 2023 and August 2023). In phase two, 65 chest pain patients were enrolled in Yichang Central People’s Hospital to test the established models in phase one (between August 2024 and Octo­ ber 2024). Coronary angiography (CAG) was performed in patients. CAD patients were defined as at least one coronary artery with stenosis of ≥ 50% in CAG. The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) history of previous Results The 16sRNA sequencing results indicated significant dysbiosis in the oral microbiota of patients with CAD, with decreased microbial abundance, network complexity and stability. The fusion model (GP-GB-SVM) demonstrated the highest performance, achieving an AUC of 0.92, ACC of 0.82, SE of 0.70, SP of 0.92, PPV of 0.88 and NPV of 0.79 in the internal test set and AUC of 0.86, ACC of 0.70, SE of 0.90, SP of 0.46, PPV of 0.60 and NPV of 0.90 in the external test set. text: t and AUC of 0.86, ACC of 0.70, SE of 0.90, SP of 0.46, PPV of 0.60 and NPV of 0.90 in the external test set. Conclusion These findings not only emphasize the significant alteration of microbiome colonized on the tongue dorsum in CAD patients but also demonstrate the tongue features associated with microbiome dysbiosis can be identified in hyperspectral images. Thereby the integration of HSI and machine learning provides novel insights into non-invasive diagnosis of CAD. Following the application of research criteria, 86 individuals were excluded, leav­ ing 190 eligible participants. All eligible participants signed informed consent for study involvement, includ­ ing voluntarism, information disclosure, decision-mak­ ing capacity, and use of their hyperspectral images and swab of tongue in research before the CAG procedure. The research protocol and consent processes received approval from the Institutional Review Board of Renmin Hospital of Wuhan University (WDRY2023-K084, chictr. org.cn identifiers ChiCTR2300073683, Registration Date:2023-7-19) and Yichang Central People’s Hospital (No.2024-216-01). The procedures in this study adhered to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki. Specimen collection and 16sRNA gene sequence The participants were divided into CAD group and con­ trol group based on the results of CAG. Tongue coating specimens were obtained in the morning before partici­ pants’ dental hygiene routines and food consumption. A tongue scraper was utilized to collect samples from the posterior to anterior regions of the tongue, repeating the process 5–7 times. The gathered coatings were placed in a 1.5 ml Eppendorf vessel containing phosphate-buffered saline solution. Subsequently, the tubes underwent cen­ trifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting super­ natant was discarded, while the precipitate was retained and preserved at -80 °C until analysis. text: and preserved at -80 °C until analysis. Bacterial DNA extraction was performed for PCR amplification using specific primers with barcodes targeting distinct regions (16SV3-V4) of 16sRNA genes. All PCR reactions were conducted using 15 µL of Phusion® High-Fidelity PCR Master Mix (New England Biolabs), 0.2 µM of forward and reverse primers, and approximately 10 ng of template DNA. The thermal cycling protocol consisted of initial denaturation at 98℃ for 1 min, followed by 30 cycles of denaturation at 98℃ for 10 s, annealing at 50℃ for 30 s, and elongation at 72℃ for 30 s, with a final extension at 72℃ for 5 min. DNA libraries were prepared per the sup­ plier’s protocol, and DNA sequencing was subsequently performed. Sequence assembly and analysis The tongue coat microbiome’s 16sRNA sequence infor­ mation from participants was utilized for comparative analysis. Sequence compilation and examination were conducted. In brief, paired-end reads were allocated to samples according to their distinct barcode and trun­ cated by removing the barcode and primer sequence. Raw tags underwent quality filtration using fastp (Version 0.23.1) software to yield high-quality Clean Tags. The tags were then evaluated against the reference database [Silva database 16 S, https://www.arb-silva.de/ Unite Database (ITS), https://unite.ut.ee/] utilizing the UCHIME ​A​l​g​o​r​i​t​ h​m (​h​t​t​p​:​/​/​w​w​w​.​d​r​i​v​e​5​.​c​o​m​/​u​s​e​a​r​c​h​/​m​a​n​u​a​l​/​u​c​h​i​m​e​_​a​l​g​ o​.​h​t​m​l) to identify chimera sequences, which were subse­ quently eliminated. Alpha diversity was computed utilizing 4 metrics in QIIME2, encompassing Chao1, Shannon, Simpson and Pielou_e to examine the diversity, richness and evenness of the communities across groups. Beta diversity was determined based on weighted and unweighted unifrac distances in QIIME2 to assess the intricacy of the com­ munity composition and contrast the disparities between CAD and control groups. Principal coordinate analysis (PCoA) was employed to elucidate the variation in micro­ bial communities between two groups utilizing weighted unifrac values. T-tests were conducted to detect differen­ tial bacteria at phylum and genus levels between the two groups. Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) effect size (LEfSe) was utilized to pinpoint key bacteria in tongue coating samples between two groups at multiple levels in datasets. The key bacteria were ranked according to the outcomes of a Mann–Whitney U-test, which ascer­ tained the features with significantly distinct abundances between assigned taxa and employed LDA to evaluate the effect size of each feature and visualize the findings using taxonomic bar charts and cladograms. The P values were adjusted as outlined by Benjamini and Hochberg. Network analysis was used to explore the symbiotic rela­ tionship between different bacterium genus and to reveal the two groups community structures. Hyperspectral image study setting Every participant contributed 25 qualified HSI images, forming the final analysis dataset. Totally 4750 images were collected to develop and test the models. text: Page 4 of 14 Li et al. BMC Cardiovascular Disorders (2025) 25:102 Before image acquisition, patients were instructed to refrain from eating and to rinse their mouths with ster­ ilized saline. The camera captured 25 spectral bands within the 688–951 nm wavelength range, featuring a spectral resolution ranging from 4 to 25 nm and a maxi­ mum spatial resolution of 644 × 484 pixels. A patient can be captured 25 images in 25 different spectral bands in a single shot. An illumination setup utilizing a 150 W quartz tungsten halogen light source was implemented. The distance between the camera’s optics and the upper surface of the tongue was 100 cm. The total image acqui­ sition time per patient was less than one second, and this rapid acquisition was generally well tolerated by the patients. In instances where tongue movement interfered with image acquisition, repeat imaging was conducted. Pre-processing and annotation of hyperspectral image data The raw hyperspectral images were subjected to initial processing to mitigate the impact of ambient light con­ ditions and sensor dark currents, following the previ­ ously described procedure [22]. The raw images were calibrated and standardized prior to subsequent analysis. The spectral and spatial features were extracted across 25 bands. The preprocessing and analysis of the HSI data were performed in ENVI5.3(The environment for visual­ izing images, ENVI, version 5.3, USA). This study centers on the prediction of CAD, as evidenced by CAG findings. Specifically, CAD is characterized as at least one coro­ nary artery with stenosis of ≥ 50% on coronary angiogra­ phy [23]. text: Page 5 of 14 Li et al. BMC Cardiovascular Disorders (2025) 25:102 patient in the training set, which encompassed charac­ teristics of the tongue dorsum. Random Forest (RF) was used in the training set for selecting the most significant HSI features to develop the models. This involves select­ ing appropriate hyperparameters and fitting the model in the training data. Subsequently, the feature impor­ tance scores were calculated and the features were ranked based on their importance scores. According to the importance scores, these features were applied to model construction until the performance of the model did not improve. To develop prediction models, fivefold cross-validation was performed in the training set with random reordering. To implement a five-fold cross-vali­ dation, the training set dataset was first randomly parti­ tioned into five subsets. The models were trained on four of these subsets and validated on the remaining subset. This process is repeated five times, with each subset serv­ ing as the validation set once. This method helps mitigate overfitting and ensures that the model generalizes well to unseen data. The prediction accuracy was determined by comparing the model outputs with the established ground truth based on CAG results. Prediction model upgradation To improve the performance of CAD prediction models, a decision-level fusion strategy was used to achieve model fusion. 30 single models were trained separately on differ­ ent feature sets in the training set. Each model outputs its prediction scores. Their predictions were aggregated at the decision level based on weighted voting. Specifically, all models were unduplicated and randomly combined in pairs, three by three until all individual models were entirely combined, for a total of 1,073,

Specifically, all models were unduplicated and randomly combined in pairs, three by three until all individual models were entirely combined, for a total of 1,073,741,793 combina­ tions. Finally, the best combination of all fusion models and individual models was selected by ROC and F1 score comparison in both external and internal test sets. Diagnostic performance of the prediction model To assess the performance of the CAD prediction mod­ els, we calculated the sensitivity (SE), specificity (SP), area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), decision curve analysis (DCA), calibration curve, positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV), accuracy (ACC) and F1 score. Statistical analysis Data are displayed as average ± standard deviation for continuous variables and proportions for categorical vari­ ables. Comparisons of continuous data were conducted using one-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test, while categorical data were evaluated using chi-square or Fisher’s exact tests. All analyses were two-tailed, with statistical significance set at P < 0.05. Statistical analyses were conducted using SPSS Statistics version 29 and Python version 3.12. Role of the funding source The funder of the study had no role in study design, data collection, data analysis, data interpretation, or writing of the report. Results Oral dysbiosis in CAD patients The swabs of the participants were analyzed using 16sRNA gene sequencing. The α diversity of the oral microbiota in CAD was significantly altered, suggesting oral dysbiosis (Fig. 2A-D). The community diversity and richness indexes, including Chao1, Pielou_e, Shannon and Simpson, showed a significant decrease in the CAD group compared to the control group. PCoA

text: dysbiosis (Fig. 2A-D). The community diversity and richness indexes, including Chao1, Pielou_e, Shannon and Simpson, showed a significant decrease in the CAD group compared to the control group. PCoA analyses revealed that there was a significant difference between the CAD group and the control group (P < 0.05) (Fig. 2E). Analysis at the phylum level showed that CAD patients presented significantly higher relative abundance of Actinobacteria, Firmicutes and Desullfobacterota (all P < 0.05), alongside a significantly lower relative abun­ dance of Proteobacteria (P < 0.001) (Fig. 2F-I). Of the nine discriminatory genera, the relative abundance of Neis­ seria and Eubacterium_yurii_group were significantly higher in the control group, and the others were signifi­ cantly higher in the CAD group, including Streptococcus, Rothia, Fusobacterium, Tannerella, Bergeyella, Lentimi­ crobium and Rikenellaceae_RC9_gut_group(Fig. 2J). LEfSe analysis was performed to identify the discrimi­ natory taxa in the tongue coating microbiota between the two groups (Supplemental Fig. 1A-B). The CAD patient was characterized by a preponderance of p_Fir­ micutes, o_Lactobacillales, c_Bacilli, f_Streptococcaceae, g_Streptococcus, o_Coriobacteriales, c_Coriobacteriia, f_Atopobiaceae and s_Actinomyces_genomosp_P1_oral_ clone_MB6_C03 [LDA score (log10) > 3], whereas the control group was characterized by a preponderance of s_Prevotellaceae_bacterium_Marseille_P2826, s_Pre­ votella_shahii, o_Sphingomonadales, g_Alloprevotella, s_Haemophilus_parainfluenzae, g_Fusobacterium, f_Fusobacteriaceae, s_Fusobacterium_periodonticum, o_Burkholderiales, g_Neisseria, f_Neisseriaceae, c_Gam­ maproteobacteria, and p_Proteobacteria [LDA score [log10] > 3]. text: Page 7 of 14 Li et al. BMC Cardiovascular Disorders (2025) 25:102 (Supplemental Fig. 2). The baseline characteristics were similar between the training and test sets. (Table 1) The training set was utilized for feature selection and model development, employing various machine learning algorithms with five-fold cross-validation. Furthermore, based on decision level fusion strategy individual model was integrated to optimize overall model performance. The predictive efficacy of these models was subsequently evaluated using the CAG imaging of the participants within the test set. Following the identification and rank­ ing of tongue dorsum HSI features utilizing random for­ ests, 30 individual prediction models were developed employing various machine learning algorithms. To fur­ ther improve model performance, a decision level fusion strategy based on the AUC was utilized to integrate indi­ vidual models. The best performing AUC combination identified was GP-GB-SVM (Fig. 4A). The analysis of spectral and texture features revealed significant dispari­ ties in the spectral and textural characteristics between the control and CAD patients (Fig. 4B). Model evaluation in internal and external test sets Totally 31 distinct machine learning algorithms were used for model development. 30 individual models were constructed and 1 fusion model was established based on decision level strategy. The six highest-perform­ ing models, as ranked by the AUC, are GP-GB-SVM (fusion model), GP, GB, SVM, RF, and TheilSen. Discussion In this study, 16sRNA sequencing of dorsal tongue swabs revealed notable dysbiosis in the oral microbiota of CAD patients. The results showed significant differences in α and β diversity between CAD and control patients. There were significant variations in relative abundance at phyla and genus levels between the two groups. Additionally, the reconstruction of microbial network was observed in CAD patients. 31 ML models based on hyperspectral images of the dorsal tongue (HSI-CAD model) were con­ structed to effectively predict CAD in patients presenting with chest pain. Among 31 prediction models assessed, the GP-GB-SVM fusion model demonstrated superior predictive performance, achieving an impressive AUC of 0.92 in the internal set and an AUC of 0.86 in the external set. The findings advocated for oral microbiome dys­ biosis in CAD patients and the implementation of this non-invasive screening technique, leveraging the remote sensing perspective to enhance the detection of CAD. Pathophysiological, the dorsal surface of the tongue in individuals diagnosed with CAD exhibits notable characteristics, as revealed by our study. Previous stud­ ies have established a strong association between oral hygiene and the development of atherosclerotic cardio­ vascular disease (ASCVD) [24, 25]. The dysbiosis of the oral microbiota plays a significant role in the forma­ tion of atherosclerotic plaques and the progression of ASCVD, consequently increasing the risk of acute myo­ cardial infarction [26]. text: Page 10 of 14 Li et al. BMC Cardiovascular Disorders (2025) 25:102 pronounced dysbiosis of the oral microbiota in patients with CAD. Significant alterations in the oral microbiota composition were identified, particularly a decrease in species abundance and reduced network complexity and stability among patients with CAD. Previous studies have demonstrated that dysbiosis of the oral microbiota can facilitate the development of arterial plaques by aug­ menting immune-mediated inflammation and perturbing lipid metabolic homeostasis [28, 29]. For instance, Fuso­ bacterium has been identified within the human arterial intima and is recognized for its role in exacerbating the progression of atherosclerosis through the promotion of inflammatory responses and the disruption of lipid metabolism [30]. The relative abundance of Fusobac­ terium was also significantly higher in CAD patients in this study. These insights suggest that changes in oral microbiota might be reflective or contributory to the pathophysiological state of CAD, presenting a holistic view of how systemic diseases can manifest in oral envi­ ronments. In addition, the results of the present study further elucidated the alterations in oral microbiota in patients with coronary artery disease compared with the results of previous studies. Firstly, we innovatively explored variations in oral microbiota in unstable angina pectoris patients. The present study was more focused on describing oral microbiota alterations in individuals at risk of coronary artery disease with symptoms of chest pain. The potential changes in oral microbiota in patients with different subtypes of coronary artery disease were further clarified. text: Page 11 of 14 Li et al. BMC Cardiovascular Disorders (2025) 25:102 imbalance of oral microenvironmental homeostasis may exist in the patients. Clinically, the diagnosis of CAD is most accurately achieved through the utilization of coronary angiogra­ phy, which is widely recognized as the gold standard in clinical practice. However, cardiac angiography is an invasive, intrusive test that carries a certain risk of surgi­ cal complications and imposes a certain financial burden on the patient [31]. If all patients with suspected coro­ nary artery disease are subjected to angiography, it will result in a waste of medical resources. In low-resource settings characterized by limited funding and inadequate cardiovascular disease screening programs, there may be a scarcity of proficient medical practitioners or appro­ priate equipment. Therefore, the implementation of a straightforward and viable auxiliary diagnostic decision system could effectively assist healthcare profession­ als in primary medical institutions to promptly identify high-risk CAD patients. Previous research has identified significant phenotypic markers in patients with CAD. For instance, Christoffersen et al. demonstrated that male pattern baldness, diagonal earlobe crease, and xanthe­ lasma significantly enhance the predictive accuracy of the Framingham model for cardiovascular disease risk [32]. In a separate study, the presence of a diagonal earlobe crease was identified as a predictor of CAD risk, demon­ strating a sensitivity of 78% and a specificity of 61% [33]. Recently, several studies have confirmed that combining deep learning algorithms with facial RGB (Red Green Blue) or near-infrared images can effectively predict CAD [12, 34]. However, previous studies have not effec­ tively addressed the confo

text: t combining deep learning algorithms with facial RGB (Red Green Blue) or near-infrared images can effectively predict CAD [12, 34]. However, previous studies have not effec­ tively addressed the confounding effects of variations in skin color across different ethnicities on superficial characteristics. Our study utilized hyperspectral images of the dorsal tongue to overcome these issues. Dorsal tongue images effectively avoid the interference of skin color differences in superficial characteristic information. Additionally, hyperspectral imaging, with its broad spec­ tral range and high data dimensionality, provides more comprehensive superficial characteristic information for CAD patients. Furthermore, previous studies of coronary artery disease prediction models were based on demo­ graphic characteristics, clinical baseline information and relevant examination results for model construction [35, 36]. In contrast, the HSI-CAD model constructed in this study applied hyperspectral features of the participants for model development without other characteristics. On the one hand, a faster screening strategy for coro­ nary artery disease was realized. On the other hand, the results suggested that specific spectral features may exist on the body surface of patients with coronary artery dis­ ease. These surface features indicate that patients may possess undetected risk factors. It provides new insights for the exploration of coronary artery disease related risk factors. Additionally, this study innovatively combined oral microbiota and hyperspectral imaging to develop a novel diagnostic tool that utilizes noninvasive spec­ tral image acquisition to explore potential alterations in patients’ oral microbiota. The GP-GB-SVM model dem­ onstrated good predictive performance (AUC:

The GP-GB-SVM model dem­ onstrated good predictive performance (AUC: 0.92 in the internal set and AUC: 0.86 in the external set) with­ out the need for baseline characteristics. The integration of HSI with the fusion model in our study facilitates the non-invasive, straightforward, and precise identifica­ tion of high-risk CAD patients, particularly in resource- limited and remote regions. The GP-GB-SVM fusion model developed in this study is expected to play a role in assisting decision-making in coronary artery disease screening. In clinical practice, the GP-GB-SVM model combined with hyperspectral features can assist physi­ cians in determining the risk of coronary artery disease and the need for further coronary angiography in high- risk patients with chest pain. With the advantages of short time-consumption and high accuracy of the fusion model, it is expected to realize the accurate identifica­ tion of high-risk patients and the exclusion of low-risk patients to reduce unnecessary examinations. In addition, some high-risk patients with chest pain may be initially evaluated in the primary care setting. The fusion model can be used to screen for the risk of coronary artery dis­ ease in these patients, and can be used to assist in early diagnosis and treatment. Furthermore, the fusion model can be used to determine the risk in patients who present with symptoms suspected to be coronary artery disease at home. The fusion model can be used to assist patients in deciding whether they need to attend hospitals to com­ plete the relevant examinations and treatments. There­ fore, this fusion model was expected to realize effective assistance in

There­ fore, this fusion model was expected to realize effective assistance in the diagnosis of CAD and decision-making of treatment strategies. And it also further confirmed the significant potential application value of artificial intelli­ gence in CAD diagnosis and treatment [37]. Overall, this study elucidated the significant changes in oral microbiota in patients with coronary artery disease, and combined hyperspectral imaging and machine learn­ ing algorithms to screen the patients’ oral characteristic spectra. On the one hand, the microbial changes in the oral cavity suggested that despite the traditional risk fac­ tors, such as diabetes and hyperlipidemia, the microbial alterations in the oral cavity may also be an important factor in the development of coronary artery disease. These patients may require not only control of traditional risk factors, but also attention to oral hygiene and health, with intervention as necessary. On the other hand, the presence of specific spectra in the oral cavity suggests microenvironmental alterations that may be caused by oral microbial and human interactions. Coronary heart disease is a disease in combination with multiple risk

text: Page 12 of 14 Li et al. BMC Cardiovascular Disorders (2025) 25:102 factors, so it is meaningful to integrate multiple risk fac­ tors to determine the degree of risk of coronary heart disease. The characteristic spectra in this study represent microenvironmental patterns that may be the result of a combination of different risk factors, such as microbial changes and metabolic variations in the human body. Therefore, these feature spectra may become new clinical biomarkers for identifying patients with coronary heart disease. However, further inclusion of more patients with different types of coronary artery disease is still needed in the future to increase the certainty of this evidence. Inclusion of more patients with different types of coro­ nary artery disease would help to further identify and clarify whether the oral microbiota of the coronary artery disease population experience changes with disease pro­ gression. Additionally, the inclusion of more patient data can be used to further optimize the HSI-CAD model and improve its clinical value. Therefore, further populariza­ tion of sequencing and hyperspectral imaging is required to promote the use of this approach in clinical practice. These technologies need to be further generalized to resource-limited areas. Secondly, more populations from different regions need to be included to clarify whether these technologies are affected by regional and popula­ tion differences. Conclusion In conclusion, oral hygiene is closely related to cardio­ vascular disease. Tongue microbiota dysbiosis can be observed in CAD patients. Therefore, further studies may need to concentrate on the changes in oral microbiota in CAD patients to clarify the exact mechanism between these alterations and CAD. text: erefore, further studies may need to concentrate on the changes in oral microbiota in CAD patients to clarify the exact mechanism between these alterations and CAD. The integration of remote sensing technology introduces a novel dimension to medical diagnostics, significantly enhancing the potential for early detection of CAD through non-invasive meth­ odologies (Fig. 6). Limitations of the study However, there are still some limitations in this study. Firstly, oral microbiome analysis was only described in the patients and did not further explore the exact mecha­ nism or potential biomarker of the special microbiome on CAD. Secondly, the study’s sample size and popula­ tion diversity may be limited, potentially impacting the generalizability of the results. It is necessary to include larger and more diverse multi-ethnic cohorts to validate the findings across different demographic groups effec­ tively. Thirdly, this study only applied machine learning algorithms for feature screening and model construction. Therefore, algorithm innovations still need to be further explored, including algorithm optimization, model effi­ ciency enhancement, model interpretation, data pro­ cessing optimization, etc. In addition, the models in this study were not constructed to incorporate baseline characteristics, and further refinement of the models by combining multiple characteristics is needed in the future. Finally, relying solely on advanced HSI technology may restrict the broader applicability of this diagnostic approach, particularly in resource-limited settings where such technology is not readily accessible.
